elements.sent-2411-001: However, observational limitations may lead to misclassifications, suggesting a larger proportion of repeaters than currently identified.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this study, we leverage unsupervised machine learning techniques to classify FRBs using data from the CHIME/FRB catalog, including both the first catalog and a recent repeater catalog.
elements.sent-2411-003: By employing Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection (UMAP) for dimensionality reduction and clustering algorithms (k-means and HDBSCAN), we successfully segregate repeaters and non-repeaters into distinct clusters, identifying over 100 potential repeater candidates.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our analysis reveals several empirical relations within the clusters, including the ${\rm log \,}\Delta t_{sc - {\rm log \,}\Delta t_{rw}$, ${\rm log \,}\Delta t_{sc - {\rm log \, T_B$, and $r - \gamma$ correlations, which provide new insights into the physical properties and emission mechanisms of FRBs.}}}
elements.sent-2411-005: This study demonstrates the effectiveness of unsupervised learning in classifying FRBs and identifying potential repeaters, paving the way for more precise investigations into their origins and applications in cosmology.
elements.sent-2411-006: Future improvements in observational data and machine learning methodologies are expected to further enhance our understanding of FRBs.
elements.sent-2411-007: Fast Radio Bursts (FRBs) are highly energetic astronomical phenomena characterized by millisecond-duration emissions.
elements.sent-2411-008: The first FRB signal was discovered in 2007 by \cite{Lorimer:2007qn , and their existence was firmly established in 2013 when \cite{Thornton:2013iua published observations of four similar events detected by the Australian Parkes Radio Telescope.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Since then, FRBs have drawn significant attention in both astronomy and cosmology \citep[e.g.,][]{Lorimer:2018rwi, Keane:2018jqo, Petroff:2021wug, Xiao:2021omr, Xiao:2021viy, Xiao:2022bbj, Zhang:2013lta, Zhang:2018gjb, Zhang:2020ass, Zhang:2021kdu, Wang:2020aut, Wang:2020rkl, Wang:2019sio, Wang:2022ami, Gao:2014iva, Qiang:2020vta, Qiang:2021bwb .}
elements.sent-2411-010: However, the origin of FRBs remains unknown.
elements.sent-2411-011: To explore their origin, numerous radio telescopes have been constructed, such as, the Deep Synoptic Array \citep[DSA;][]{DSA-10, DSA-2000 , Arecibo \citep{Spitler:2014fla , Parkes \citep[e.g.,][]{Lorimer:2007qn, Burke-Spolaor:2014rqa, Petroff:2015bua, Ravi:2014gxa , the Canadian Hydrogen Intensity Mapping Experiment \citep[CHIME;][]{CHIMEFRB-IM , the Five-hundred-meter Aperture Spherical Radio Telescope \citep[FAST;][]{FAST-IM , and the Australian Square Kilometer Array Pathfinder \citep[ASKAP;][]{ASKAP-IM .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: So far, nearly a thousand FRBs have been observed\citep{Petroff:2016tcr, CHIMEFRBcat1, Jankowski:2023sot, Xu:2023did , with nearly 100 of them have known redshifts \citep[e.g.][]{DSA-110_cat1, DSA-110_cat2, Bhardwaj2024ApJ, Gordon2023ApJ .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: To date, the largest FRB sample is the first CHIME/FRB catalog \citep{CHIMEFRBcat1 .}
elements.sent-2411-014: In astronomical observations, the measured quantities are often influenced by the distance between the source and Earth.
elements.sent-2411-015: Therefore, distance-related information is crucial for analyzing the origin of FRBs.
elements.sent-2411-016: A key observational parameter correlated with distance for FRBs is the Dispersion Measure (DM), which quantifies the total column density of free electrons along the line of sight between the source and the observer.
elements.sent-2411-017: For the vast majority of observed FRBs, the DM values significantly exceed the predicted values for their respective directions within the Milky Way, indicating that these FRBs originate from distant extragalactic regions of the universe.
elements.sent-2411-018: Meanwhile, an FRB event, FRB 20200428, was observed to have originated within the Milky Way, providing evidence that at least some FRBs are associated with magnetars \citep{CHIMEFRB:2020abu, Insight-HXMTTeam:2020dmu, Bochenek:2020zxn, Lin:2020mpw .}
elements.sent-2411-019: Based on the observational characteristics, FRBs are broadly classified into two categories, i.e., repeaters and non-repeaters.
elements.sent-2411-020: As the names suggest, repeaters are sources that have exhibited multiple bursts, whereas non-repeaters have only been observed once.
elements.sent-2411-021: Since the discovery of the first repeater, FRB 20121102 \citep{Spitler:2016dmz , more than 50 FRBs have been identified as repeaters to date \citep{ CHIMEFRBcat1, CHIMEFRB2023, Kumar2019ApJ, Kirsten:2021llv, Fonseca:2020cdd, Niu:2021bnl, Xu2022Natur , while most of the remaining ones are classified as non-repeaters.}}
elements.sent-2411-022: However, \cite{Ravi:2019iop suggests that the volumetric rate of non-repeating FRBs might exceed that of cataclysmic events and the formation rate of compact objects, implying that the majority of FRBs should be repeaters.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Some studies also propose that more than half of the FRBs in the first CHIME/FRB catalog could be repeaters \citep{Yamasaki:2023dlb, McGregor:2023mzr .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Furthermore, several FRBs initially identified as non-repeaters were later observed to show repeating characteristics \citep{CHIMEFRB2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-025: This suggests that many of the FRBs currently classified as non-repeaters might actually be potential repeaters, with only a single burst detected due to various observational factors.
elements.sent-2411-026: As a result, some studies have attempted to identify potential repeaters among apparent non-repeating FRBs, and one of the methods being employed is machine learning.
elements.sent-2411-027: Machine learning is an artificial intelligence technique that allows computers to learn from data and make predictions or decisions without being explicitly programmed \citep{ml1967, ml1986 .}
elements.sent-2411-028: Machine learning is generally categorized into three types, i.e., supervised learning, unsupervised learning, and semi-supervised learning \citep[e.g.][]{ml1, ml2, ml3, ml4 .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Using algorithms to uncover patterns in the data can be applied to tasks such as classification, regression, clustering, and optimization.
elements.sent-2411-030: Until now, machine learning has already been widely used in the detection and analysis of FRBs \citep[e.g.][]{Wagstaff2016PASP, Zhang2018ApJ, Wu2019ApJ, Yang2021MNRAS, Adamek2020ApJS, Agarwal2020MNRAS, Bhatporia2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-031: For instance within the first CHIME/FRB catalog dataset, \cite{Chen:2021jpq and \cite{Zhu-Ge:2022nkz identify 188 and 117 repeater candidates from 474 non-repeating FRBs via unsupervised learning, correspondingly.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Furthermore, \cite{Luo:2022smj found that the most prominent factors to distinguish between non-repeating and repeating FRBs are brightness temperature and rest-frame frequency bandwidth, whereas \cite{Sun:2024huw found spectral running may play a role instead.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: Furthermore, some studies have used machine learning techniques to classify thousands of bursts from highly active repeaters, such as FRB 20121102 \citep{Raquel:2023tgi and FRB 20201124A \citep{Chen:2023pub , in an effort to analyze their potential radiation mechanisms.}}
elements.sent-2411-034: In addition to classifying FRBs based on their repeatability, some studies have also investigated the clustering of FRBs using characteristics beyond repeatability, as well as the empirical relationships among these features.
elements.sent-2411-035: For instance, similar to the well-known classification of GRB, where short GRBs are short lived and associated with old population, while long GRB with long duration and associated with young population \citep{grb1, grb2 , \cite{Guo:2022wpf proposed classifying FRBs based on their association with either old or young stellar populations.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Many empirical relations still hold for localized FRBs \citep{Li2024arXiv240812983 .}
elements.sent-2411-039: Based on these empirical relations, FRBs could potentially serve as standard candles, allowing cosmological models to be constrained without relying on DM measurements \citep{Guo:2023hgb .}
elements.sent-2411-040: A strong power-law correlation between fluence and peak flux density was identified for these categories.
elements.sent-2411-041: A tight power-law correlation between pulse width and fluence was also observed for classical bursts.
elements.sent-2411-042: CHIME has recently released a new repeater catalog \citep{CHIMEFRB2023 , significantly increasing the data available on repeaters.}
elements.sent-2411-043: This expanded dataset is expected to improve the accuracy of identifying repeater candidates through machine learning.
elements.sent-2411-044: In this paper, we will apply unsupervised learning methods to classify the FRBs from the combined data of these two catalogs into different clusters, find potential repeaters among the non-repeaters, and analyze possible empirical relations across these clusters.
elements.sent-2411-045: This paper is structured as follows.
elements.sent-2411-046: In Section \ref{sec:data , we present the selected CHIME data and the features used in our analysis.}
elements.sent-2411-047: In Section \ref{sec:res , we present the results of dimensionality reduction and clustering, then we analyze the potential empirical relations.}

elements.sent-2411-055: Based on these data, we choose the parameters of the sub-bursts as the features for machine learning algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-056: To provide a more comprehensive description of an FRB event, we selected as many parameters as possible to serve as features.
elements.sent-2411-057: These parameters can be divided into two categories: observational parameters (original data provided by Cat1 and Cat2023) and derived parameters, and we present the distribution of all parameters for the entire FRB dataset in Figure \ref{fig:fd .}
elements.sent-2411-058: We chose 10 observational parameters as did by \cite{Chen2022 :.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Boxcar width \bcwidth\,(ms) -- The boxcar width of sub-burst, with the label name `bc\_width' in two catalogs.
elements.sent-2411-060: Width of sub-burst \widthfitb\,(ms) -- The width of sub-burst that is fitted by fitburst\,\footnote{\url{https://github.com/CHIMEFRB/fitburst , with the label name `width\_fitb' in two catalogs.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: Flux \flux\,(Jy) -- The peak flux of the band-average profile (lower limit) with the label name `flux' in two catalogs.
elements.sent-2411-062: Fluence \fluence\,($\rm Jy \cdot ms$) -- The flux integrated over the duration of sub-burst (lower limit) with the label name `fluence' in two catalogs.
elements.sent-2411-063: Scattering time \scattime\,(ms) -- The scattering time at 600 MHz of each sub-burst, with the label name `scat\_time' in two catalogs.
elements.sent-2411-064: Spectral index \spidx \ -- The spectral shape parameter of sub-burst.
elements.sent-2411-065: The label name in two catalogs is `sp\_idx'.
elements.sent-2411-066: Spectral running \sprun \ -- This value characterizes the frequency dependence of the spectral shape and is labeled as `sp\_run' in both catalogs.
elements.sent-2411-067: Highest frequency \highfreq\,(MHz) -- The highest frequency band of detection for the sub-burst at full-width tenth-maximum.
elements.sent-2411-068: The label name in two catalogs is `high\_freq'.
elements.sent-2411-069: Lowest frequency \lowfreq\,(MHz) -- The lowest frequency band of detection for the sub-burst at full-width tenth-maximum.
elements.sent-2411-070: The label name in two catalogs is `low\_freq'.
elements.sent-2411-071: Peak frequency \peakfreq\,(MHz) -- The peak frequency for the sub-burst and is labeled as `peak\_freq' in both catalogs.
elements.sent-2411-072: For \bcwidth, \widthfitb, \flux, \fluence, and \scattime, we take their logarithmic values throughout this work.
elements.sent-2411-073: Cat1 and Cat2023 only provide upper limits for the width of sub-burst and scattering time for some FRBs, so we opted to use these upper limits in our analysis.

elements.sent-2411-094: Dimensionality reduction and clustering are two types of unsupervised machine learning methods used in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-095: First, we apply the dimensionality reduction algorithm to automatically convert high-dimensional data into low-dimensional data.
elements.sent-2411-096: Then, we use a clustering algorithm to classify the reduced-dimensional data based on their similarities.
elements.sent-2411-097: In this study, we use Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection \citep[UMAP,][]{umap , implemented via the python package umap-learn\,\footnote{\url{https://github.com/lmcinnes/umap , to perform dimensionality reduction.}}}
elements.sent-2411-098: UMAP is a dimensionality reduction technique that can be used for both visualization and general nonlinear dimensionality reduction.
elements.sent-2411-099: This algorithm assumes that the input data is uniformly distributed on a Riemannian manifold with a locally constant (or approximately constant) Riemannian metric and that the manifold is locally connected.
elements.sent-2411-100: Based on these assumptions, the manifold can be modeled using a fuzzy topological structure.
elements.sent-2411-101: The use of UMAP has been extensively explored in many studies.
elements.sent-2411-102: For FRB classification, the three parameters n\_compnent, n\_neighbors, and min\_dist have a more significant impact on the classification results.
elements.sent-2411-103: Meanwhile, we also experimented with modifying other parameters and found that they had minimal effect on the classification outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-104: Therefore, in this study, we choose to focus on adjusting these three parameters of UMAP.
elements.sent-2411-105: In our work, we set $\mathtt{n\_compnent =2$ for all features, projecting the data onto a 2D plane for visual representation.n\_neighbors controls how UMAP balance the local and globol structure of data.}
elements.sent-2411-106: UMAP achieves this by controlling the size of the local neighborhood it considers when attempting to learn the underlying structure of the data.
elements.sent-2411-107: This means that low values of n\_neighbors will cause UMAP to focus on very local structures, potentially at the cost of missing the overall global structure.
elements.sent-2411-108: On the other hand, higher values of n\_neighbors will push UMAP to consider larger neighborhoods around each point, capturing the broader structure of the data, but possibly losing finer details.
elements.sent-2411-109: Basically, it sets the minimum distance between points in the low-dimensional space.
elements.sent-2411-110: Lower values of min\_dist will lead to more tightly packed, ``clumpier" embeddings, which can be beneficial for identifying clusters or preserving finer topological details.
elements.sent-2411-111: In contrast, higher values of min\_dist will prevent the points from being tightly packed, instead focusing on maintaining the broader topological structure.
elements.sent-2411-112: We scan n\_neighbors from 2 to 50 and min\_dist from 0.0 to 0.99.
elements.sent-2411-113: In this paper, we take n\_neighbors = 21, and min\_dist = 0.03.
elements.sent-2411-114: In this work, we used k-means \citep{macqueen1967_k-means,lloyd1982_k-means and Hierarchical Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise \citep[HDBSCAN][]{Campello_2013_HDBSCAN,Campello2015-HDBSCAN,McInnes2017_HDBSCAN to group the reduced-dimensional data into different clusters.}}
elements.sent-2411-115: K-means clustering is based on the distance between each data point and its corresponding cluster center.
elements.sent-2411-116: It aims to minimize the distance between the points and their respective centers, effectively grouping similar points into clusters based on proximity in the feature space.
elements.sent-2411-117: Initially, the k-means algorithm selects k random points as the initial cluster centers, calculates the Euclidean distance of each data point from these centers, and assigns each point to the nearest center.
elements.sent-2411-118: Then, it recalculates the mean of each cluster, updating the cluster centers.
elements.sent-2411-119: This process is repeated iteratively until the cluster centers stabilize, minimizing the overall variance within the cluster \citep[for detals, see][and reference their in]{umlrevi .}
elements.sent-2411-120: We use sklearn.cluster.KMeans\,\footnote{\url{https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.cluster.KMeans.html to perform the k-means clustering algorethm.}}
elements.sent-2411-121: The essential hyperparameter is n\_clusters, it means how many clusters are present in the model.
elements.sent-2411-122: HDBSCAN is a clustering algorithm developed by \cite{Campello_2013_HDBSCAN, Campello2015-HDBSCAN .}
elements.sent-2411-123: It extends Density-based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise \citep[DBSCAN, ][]{ester1996_dbscan by transforming it into a hierarchical clustering method \citep{HAN2012443 .}}
elements.sent-2411-124: The algorithm then extracts flat clusters from this hierarchy based on the stability of the clusters, which allows it to better handle varying densities and identify clusters of different shapes and sizes.
elements.sent-2411-125: HDBSCAN uses minimum spanning trees, allowing it to discover clusters with varying densities, unlike DBSCAN, which assumes a constant density across the entire dataset.
elements.sent-2411-126: This flexibility enables HDBSCAN to identify clusters of different shapes and sizes, making it more adaptable to complex structures.
elements.sent-2411-127: We use a python package hdbscan\,\footnote{\url{https://hdbscan.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html to perform this clustering algorithm.}}
elements.sent-2411-128: The hyperparameters of HDBSCAN adjusted in this paper are min\_cluster\_size and min\_samples.
elements.sent-2411-129: These parameters directly influence the number of clusters and the overall shape of the clustering.

elements.sent-2411-130: To improve the clustering results, we optimize the hyperparameters of the clustering algorithm by maximizing the mean silhouette coefficient\,\footnote{\url{https://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.metrics.silhouette_score.html \citep{silhouettes across all samples.}}}
elements.sent-2411-131: The silhouette coefficient measures how well a sample fits within its assigned cluster, with values ranging from -1 to 1.
elements.sent-2411-132: Higher values indicate more tightly grouped, well-defined clusters.
elements.sent-2411-133: We show the mean silhouette coefficients changed with the hyperparameters of the clustering algorithm in Figure \ref{fig:sch .}

elements.sent-2411-135: However, the number of noise points increased during this range.
elements.sent-2411-136: Therefore, we chose min\_cluster\_size = 37 for this study.

elements.sent-2411-204: Machine learning is a powerful tool to classify FRBs.
elements.sent-2411-205: In this paper, we applied unsupervised learning methods, including dimensionality reduction and clustering algorithms, to differentiate between repeaters and non-repeaters in the first CHIME/FRB catalog \citep{CHIMEFRBcat1 and the CHIME/FRB Collaboration (2023) catalog \citep{CHIMEFRB2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-206: We extracted 16 parameters from the FRBs to serve as input features for unsupervised learning, ensuring that the information from the FRBs was sufficiently comprehensive.
elements.sent-2411-207: Ultimately, we successfully identified several candidate repeaters among the non-repeaters.
elements.sent-2411-208: Using the UMAP+k-means method, we identified 269 non-repeaters as repeater candidates, with an estimated repeating rate of 61.7\%.

elements.sent-2411-231: Fast Radio Bursts (FRBs) are highly energetic millisecond-duration astrophysical phenomena typically categorized as repeaters or non-repeaters.
elements.sent-2411-232: However, observational limitations may result in misclassifications, potentially leading to a higher proportion of repeaters than currently identified.
elements.sent-2411-233: In this study, we leverage unsupervised machine learning techniques to classify FRBs using data from the CHIME/FRB catalogs, including both the first catalog and a recent repeater catalog.
elements.sent-2411-234: By employing Uniform Manifold Approximation and Projection (UMAP) for dimensionality reduction and clustering algorithms (k-means and Hierarchical Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise [HDBSCAN]), we successfully segregate repeaters and non-repeaters into distinct clusters, identifying over 100 potential repeater candidates.
elements.sent-2411-235: Our analysis reveals several empirical relations within the clusters, including the ${\rm log \,}\Delta t_{sc - {\rm log \,}\Delta t_{rw}$, ${\rm log \,}\Delta t_{sc - {\rm log \, T_B$, and $r - \gamma$ correlations, where ${\Delta t_{sc , \Delta t_{rw , T_B, r, \gamma}$ represent scattering time, rest-frame width, brightness temperature, spectral running, and spectral index, respectively.}}}}}
